message.content:
Do a very thorough exploration of the project at C:\BudgetMart. I need a complete picture of:

1. Overall project structure (all directories and files)
2. What the project does (purpose, features)
3. Tech stack (frontend, backend, APIs, databases, deployment)
4. All Python/backend files - read their full content
5. All frontend files (HTML, CSS, JS) - read their full content
6. Configuration files (requirements.txt, vercel.json, package.json, .env files, etc.)
7. Any README or documentation
8. API routes and endpoints
9. How data flows through the system
10. Any external APIs used

Read every non-trivial file completely. This is for writing a comprehensive explanation document. Search breadth: very thorough.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# 🚀 Deploying BudgetMart on Vercel

Since Render now requires a credit card, we will use **Vercel** instead! Vercel is incredibly fast, completely free, and does **not** require a credit card for GitHub signups. 

We will deploy both the Frontend and the Backend to Vercel at the same time.

## Step 1: Push to GitHub
I have already re-configured your files for Vercel. You just need to push them:
1. Open your terminal in VS Code (or PowerShell).
2. Run: `git add .`
3. Run: `git commit -m "Configure for Vercel deployment"`
4. Run: `git push origin main`

---

## Step 2: Deploy to Vercel
1. Go to [Vercel.com](https://v                                                                         ercel.com/) and sign up/log in using your **GitHub account**.
2. Click the black **Add New...** button and select **Project**.
3. You will see a list of your GitHub repositories. Find `budgetmart` and click **Import**.

### Step 3: Configure Settings
On the project configuration page, make the following precise changes:

1. **Framework Preset:** Leave as `Other`.
2. **Root Directory:** Click "Edit", select the `backend` folder, and save.
   *(We deploy the backend first, then we will deploy the frontend separately).*
3. **Environment Variables:** 
   - Expand this section.
   - **Name:** `SERPAPI_KEY`
   - **Value:** `YOUR_SERPAPI_KEY_HERE` (Get a new one from your SerpApi dashboard!)
   - Click **Add**.
4. Click the big **Deploy** button.

---

### Step 4: Link Frontend to the Deployed Backend
1. Once Vercel finishes deploying the backend, it will give you a domain (e.g., `https://budgetmart-backend-something.vercel.app`). **Copy that URL.**
2. Open your local `frontend/script.js` file.
3. Replace the placeholder URL with your actual Vercel backend URL:
   ```javascript
   const API_URL = window.location.hostname === '127.0.0.1' || window.location.hostname === 'localhost' 
       ? 'http://127.0.0.1:5000' 
       : 'https://YOUR_NEW_VERCEL_BACKEND_URL.vercel.app'; // <--- PASTE HERE
   ```
4. Save the file, commit, and push again:
   - `git add frontend/script.js`
   - `git commit -m "Update API URL for prod"`
   - `git push origin main`

### Step 5: Deploy the Frontend
1. Go back to your Vercel Dashboard and click **Add New... > Project** again.
2. Import the `budgetmart` repository again.
3. This time, change the **Root Directory** to `frontend`.
4. Click **Deploy**.

**You're done!** Vercel will give you a new link for the frontend, and your full-stack app will be live and communicating perfectly without requiring any credit cards.

import re

def normalize_product_name(query: str) -> str:
    """
    Normalizes a product name by lowercasing, stripping extra whitespace,
    and removing common filler words or standardizing units.
    """
    query = query.lower().strip()
    # Replace common variations
    query = query.replace("1/2 litre", "500ml")
    query = query.replace("half litre", "500ml")
    query = query.replace("1 kg", "1000g")
    
    # Remove extra spaces
    query = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', query)
    
    return query

def generate_mock_data(query: str, platform_name: str, base_price: float, stock_chance: float = 0.9) -> dict:
    """
    Generates realistic mock data for the given platform.
    """
    import random
    
    # Simulate an Out of Stock scenario
    is_in_stock = random.random() < stock_chance
    
    # Introduce small price variations between platforms (+/- 10%)
    price_variation = base_price * random.uniform(-0.1, 0.1)
    final_price = round(base_price + price_variation)
    
    return {
        "platform": platform_name,
        "product_name": query.title(),
        "price": final_price if is_in_stock else None,
        "stock": is_in_stock,
        "product_url": f"https://www.{platform_name.lower()}.com/search?q={query.replace(' ', '+')}"
    }

def get_base_pricing(normalized_query: str) -> float:
    """
    Determine a basic authentic fake base price based on item keywords.
    """
    db = {
        "milk": 30,
        "amul milk": 33,
        "bread": 40,
        "britannia bread": 45,
        "butter": 55,
        "amul butter": 60,
        "eggs": 70,
        "coffee": 150,
        "nescafe": 200,
        "maggi": 14,
        "yippee": 12,
        "rice": 60,
        "basmati rice": 120,
        "flour": 40,
        "sugar": 45,
        "salt": 25,
        "tata salt": 28,
        "chips": 20,
        "lays": 20,
        "kurkure": 20,
        "coke": 40,
        "pepsi": 38
    }
    
    # Try exact match
    if normalized_query in db:
        return db[normalized_query]
        
    # Try partial match
    for key, price in db.items():
        if key in normalized_query:
            return price
            
    # Default base price for an unknown item
    import random
    return random.randint(30, 250)

from serpapi import GoogleSearch
import asyncio
import re
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

API_KEY = os.getenv("SERPAPI_KEY", "")

def fetch_real_shopping_data(query: str, location: str = "India") -> list[dict]:
    """
    Fetches real-time pricing and availability from Google Shopping using SerpApi.
    Supports a location string (e.g. "Mumbai", "110001", "India").
    """
    params = {
      "engine": "google_shopping",
      "q": query,
      "api_key": API_KEY,
      "gl": "in",
      "hl": "en",
      "location": location,
    }
    
    try:
        search = GoogleSearch(params)
        results = search.get_dict()
        shopping_results = results.get("shopping_results", [])

        
        parsed_data = []
        for item in shopping_results[:12]:  # Limit to top 12 results
            # Extract numbers from price if necessary, though extracted_price gives floats
            price = item.get("extracted_price")
            source = item.get("source", "Unknown Store")
            
            # Simple stock logic: if there's a price, it's generally in stock, unless explicitly stated otherwise
            # SerpApi doesn't always expose direct "OOS" unless in delivery text. We assume True.
            
            # Use direct merchant link if available, fallback to google shopping product link
            product_url = item.get("link") or item.get("product_link") or "#"
            
            # Extract quantity
            raw_title = item.get("title", query)
            found_qty = []
            
            # Match packs/sets first
            pack_match = re.search(r'((?:pack|set)\s+of\s+\d+)', raw_title, re.IGNORECASE)
            if pack_match:
                found_qty.append(pack_match.group(1).title())
                
            # Match standard units (weight/volume/pieces)
            weight_match = re.search(r'(\d+(?:\.\d+)?\s*(?:ml|l|litre|litres|g|kg|gm|piece|pieces|pc|pcs|unit|units))\b', raw_title, re.IGNORECASE)
            if weight_match:
                found_qty.append(weight_match.group(1).lower().strip())
                
            quantity_str = " | ".join(found_qty)
            
            parsed_data.append({
                "platform": source,
                "product_name": raw_title,
                "quantity": quantity_str,
                "price": price,
                "delivery": item.get("delivery", "Fees calculated at checkout"),
                "stock": True if price else False,
                "product_url": product_url,
                "image_url": item.get("thumbnail", "")
            })
            
        return parsed_data
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"SerpApi Error: {e}")
        return []

import re

def extract_quantity(title):
    # Regex to find quantity patterns like 500ml, 1 kg, 200 g, 1L, etc.
    # Matches a number (with optional decimal), optional space, and a unit.
    pattern = r'(\d+(?:\.\d+)?\s*(?:ml|l|litre|litres|g|kg|gm|piece|pieces|pc|pcs|pack|pk|unit|units))\b'
    match = re.search(pattern, title, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        return match.group(1).lower().strip()
    return "1 Unit" # Fallback

titles = [
    "Amul Milk 500 ml",
    "Britannia Good Day 600g",
    "Onion 1kg",
    "Amul Slim N Trim Skimmed Milk",
    "Fortune Refined Soyabean Oil 1 Litre",
    "Eggs 6 pieces",
    "Apples 500 gm",
    "Coca Cola 2.25L"
]

for t in titles:
    print(f"{t} -> {extract_quantity(t)}")

1	# BudgetMart Technical Interview Preparation
2	
3	This document is structured to help you intuitively understand the core concepts behind your BudgetMart project and give clear, confident answers during a technical interview.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Complete Architecture (Flow)
8	**The Flow (Frontend → Backend → API → Response):**
9	1. **Frontend:** The User types a query (e.g., "Milk") and clicks search. The Javascript code makes an HTTP GET request to the Backend.
10	2. **Backend (API):** Your Flask app receives the request at the `/search` route. 
11	3. **Data Fetching:** The backend calls the external SerpAPI (Google Shopping API) using your secured API key to fetch real-time data.
12	4. **Processing & Caching:** The backend extracts useful data from the messy SerpAPI response (like price, name, quantity), caches it for 5 minutes (to save API credits), and sends a clean JSON array back to the frontend.
13	5. **Render:** The frontend receives the JSON and dynamically generates HTML cards for each product on the screen.
14	
15	---
16	
17	## 2. Frontend Files
18	* **`index.html` (The Skeleton):** Defines the structure of the web page. It contains the search form, input fields (product, location), sorting dropdown, and a placeholder container (`<div id="resultsGrid">`) where the results will be injected.
19	* **`style.css` (The Look):** Applies the visual styling. It handles the color scheme (glassmorphism/blobs), typography (Outfit font), grid layouts, and smooth animations (like cards appearing).
20	* **`script.js` (The Brains):** Handles the DOM manipulation. It listens for form submissions, shows/hides the loading spinner, makes the API call to the backend, sorts the data, and loops through the results to create HTML cards dynamically. 
21	
22	---
23	
24	## 3. How API Calls Are Made (Frontend)
25	* We use the built-in browser API called `fetch`.
26	* We use **`async/await`** to make the code asynchronous but readable like synchronous code. 
27	* First, we do `const response = await fetch(url)`. JavaScript pauses this function here until the backend responds.
28	* Then we do `const data = await response.json()`. This converts the backend's JSON string into JavaScript objects (arrays and dictionaries) that we can loop over easily.
29	* We wrap it in a `try...catch` block so if the server crashes or the internet drops, we can show an error message on the screen gracefully.
30	
31	---
32	
33	## 4. Backend Structure (Flask)
34	* **Flask App (`index.py`):** The entry point. Initializes Flask, enables CORS (so the frontend on a different port can communicate with it), sets up the Cache, and defines the API endpoints (`/` home, `/health`, and `/search`).
35	* **`services/` Folder:** This holds the modular business logic. Instead of putting hundreds of lines of code in `index.py`, we separate concerns.
36	* **`serp_service.py`:** A dedicated file containing the logic to talk to SerpAPI and clean the results.
37	* **`normalizer.py`:** Contains helper functions (like mapping standard base prices or normalizing messy product names).
38	
39	---
40	
41	## 5. How `/search` Endpoint Works Step-by-Step
42	1. **Receive:** The user hits `/search?product=Milk&location=India`.
43	2. **Extract:** Flask extracts `product` and `location` from `request.args`. If `product` is empty, it returns a `400 Bad Request`.
44	3. **Check Cache:** It checks if this exact query was searched in the last 5 minutes. If yes, it returns the stored result immediately.
45	4. **Fetch Data:** It calls `fetch_real_shopping_data(query, location)` from `serp_service.py`.
46	5. **Validation:** If the array is empty, it returns a `404 Not Found` error.
47	6. **Respond:** It returns the Python dictionary as JSON using `jsonify(results)`, with a `200 OK` status.
48	
49	---
50	
51	## 6. SerpAPI Integration
52	* SerpAPI is essentially a web scraper API for Google. 
53	* In `serp_service.py`, we initialize `GoogleSearch(params)` passing our API key, the query `q`, location details, and `engine: "google_shopping"`.
54	* We slice the result `shopping_results[:12]` to get just the top 12 items.
55	* We loop over the items and extract what we need: `extracted_price` (which is a float), `source` (platform name), product URLs, and image links.
56	
57	---
58	
59	## 7. Caching (Flask-Caching) Internals
60	* **Why?** SerpAPI limits how many requests you can make per month. If 10 users search "Milk" in 1 minute, we don't want to waste 10 SerpAPI calls.
61	* **How?** We use `Flask-Caching` set to `SimpleCache` (stores the data in the server's RAM).
62	* We use the decorator `@cache.cached(query_string=True)` on the route. It uses the URL path (e.g., `?product=Milk`) as the dictionary "key", and the JSON response as the "value". 
63	* It's configured with a 300-second (5 minute) timeout. After 5 minutes, the key expires.
64	
65	---
66	
67	## 8. Normalization and Quantity Extraction (Regex)
68	* Google Shopping titles are messy (e.g., "Amul Fresh Milk - 500 ml Pack of 2"). 
69	* In `serp_service.py`, we use **Regex (Regular Expressions)** to pull out quantities.
70	* First, it looks for "pack of X" using `r'((?:pack|set)\s+of\s+\d+)'`.
71	* Then, it looks for weight/volume using `r'(\d+(?:\.\d+)?\s*(?:ml|l|g|kg|pcs|pieces))\b'`. 
72	* If found, it combines them (e.g., "Pack Of 2 | 500 ml") and saves them to the custom `quantity` attribute, so the frontend UI can display it neatly.
73	
74	---
75	
76	## 9. Error Handling
77	* **Backend Errors:** If SerpAPI fails or the key is wrong, the Python `try...except` block in `serp_service.py` catches it and returns an empty list `[]`. The route sees this empty list and returns a clean `{ "error": "No matching product found" }` with a 404 status. If no product query is passed, it safely returns 400.
78	* **Frontend Errors:** If `response.ok` is false (meaning the status code isn't 200), the frontend throws a Javascript error, catching the backend's message. The Catch block kicks in, hides the loading spinner, and displays the error message in red on the UI.
79	
80	---
81	
82	## 10. Deployment on Vercel (Serverless)
83	* Usually, a backend server constantly runs 24/7 waiting for requests.
84	* Vercel uses **Serverless Functions**. When you mapped `api/index.py` in `vercel.json`, you turned your Flask app into a lambda function.
85	* **How it works:** Vercel puts the backend to "sleep". When a user hits the endpoint, Vercel quickly spins up an instance just for that single request, runs the Python code, returns the JSON, and shuts down again. This is incredibly cost-effective.
86	
87	---
88	
89	## 11. Possible Interview Questions
90	1. How does your frontend communicate with the backend?
91	2. What is CORS and why did you need it in Flask?
92	3. If Google Shopping changes its title formats, how does it affect your app?
93	4. How did you optimize the SerpAPI rate limits?
94	5. Why did you choose `async/await` instead of `.then()` promises?
95	6. Tell me about Serverless architecture. What is a "cold start"?
96	
97	---
98	
99	## 12. Short Answers to Practice Speaking
100	* **Q: How does frontend talk to backend?**

# BudgetMart Technical Interview Preparation

This document is structured to help you intuitively understand the core concepts behind your BudgetMart project and give clear, confident answers during a technical interview.

---

## 1. Complete Architecture (Flow)
**The Flow (Frontend → Backend → API → Response):**
1. **Frontend:** The User types a query (e.g., "Milk") and clicks search. The Javascript code makes an HTTP GET request to the Backend.
2. **Backend (API):** Your Flask app receives the request at the `/search` route. 
3. **Data Fetching:** The backend calls the external SerpAPI (Google Shopping API) using your secured API key to fetch real-time data.
4. **Processing & Caching:** The backend extracts useful data from the messy SerpAPI response (like price, name, quantity), caches it for 5 minutes (to save API credits), and sends a clean JSON array back to the frontend.
5. **Render:** The frontend receives the JSON and dynamically generates HTML cards for each product on the screen.

---

## 2. Frontend Files
* **`index.html` (The Skeleton):** Defines the structure of the web page. It contains the search form, input fields (product, location), sorting dropdown, and a placeholder container (`<div id="resultsGrid">`) where the results will be injected.
* **`style.css` (The Look):** Applies the visual styling. It handles the color scheme (glassmorphism/blobs), typography (Outfit font), grid layouts, and smooth animations (like cards appearing).
* **`script.js` (The Brains):** Handles the DOM manipulation. It listens for form submissions, shows/hides the loading spinner, makes the API call to the backend, sorts the data, and loops through the results to create HTML cards dynamically. 

---

## 3. How API Calls Are Made (Frontend)
* We use the built-in browser API called `fetch`.
* We use **`async/await`** to make the code asynchronous but readable like synchronous code. 
* First, we do `const response = await fetch(url)`. JavaScript pauses this function here until the backend responds.
* Then we do `const data = await response.json()`. This converts the backend's JSON string into JavaScript objects (arrays and dictionaries) that we can loop over easily.
* We wrap it in a `try...catch` block so if the server crashes or the internet drops, we can show an error message on the screen gracefully.

---

## 4. Backend Structure (Flask)
* **Flask App (`index.py`):** The entry point. Initializes Flask, enables CORS (so the frontend on a different port can communicate with it), sets up the Cache, and defines the API endpoints (`/` home, `/health`, and `/search`).
* **`services/` Folder:** This holds the modular business logic. Instead of putting hundreds of lines of code in `index.py`, we separate concerns.
* **`serp_service.py`:** A dedicated file containing the logic to talk to SerpAPI and clean the results.
* **`normalizer.py`:** Contains helper functions (like mapping standard base prices or normalizing messy product names).

---

## 5. How `/search` Endpoint Works Step-by-Step
1. **Receive:** The user hits `/search?product=Milk&location=India`.
2. **Extract:** Flask extracts `product` and `location` from `request.args`. If `product` is empty, it returns a `400 Bad Request`.
3. **Check Cache:** It checks if this exact query was searched in the last 5 minutes. If yes, it returns the stored result immediately.
4. **Fetch Data:** It calls `fetch_real_shopping_data(query, location)` from `serp_service.py`.
5. **Validation:** If the array is empty, it returns a `404 Not Found` error.
6. **Respond:** It returns the Python dictionary as JSON using `jsonify(results)`, with a `200 OK` status.

---

## 6. SerpAPI Integration
* SerpAPI is essentially a web scraper API for Google. 
* In `serp_service.py`, we initialize `GoogleSearch(params)` passing our API key, the query `q`, location details, and `engine: "google_shopping"`.
* We slice the result `shopping_results[:12]` to get just the top 12 items.
* We loop over the items and extract what we need: `extracted_price` (which is a float), `source` (platform name), product URLs, and image links.

---

## 7. Caching (Flask-Caching) Internals
* **Why?** SerpAPI limits how many requests you can make per month. If 10 users search "Milk" in 1 minute, we don't want to waste 10 SerpAPI calls.
* **How?** We use `Flask-Caching` set to `SimpleCache` (stores the data in the server's RAM).
* We use the decorator `@cache.cached(query_string=True)` on the route. It uses the URL path (e.g., `?product=Milk`) as the dictionary "key", and the JSON response as the "value". 
* It's configured with a 300-second (5 minute) timeout. After 5 minutes, the key expires.

---

## 8. Normalization and Quantity Extraction (Regex)
* Google Shopping titles are messy (e.g., "Amul Fresh Milk - 500 ml Pack of 2"). 
* In `serp_service.py`, we use **Regex (Regular Expressions)** to pull out quantities.
* First, it looks for "pack of X" using `r'((?:pack|set)\s+of\s+\d+)'`.
* Then, it looks for weight/volume using `r'(\d+(?:\.\d+)?\s*(?:ml|l|g|kg|pcs|pieces))\b'`. 
* If found, it combines them (e.g., "Pack Of 2 | 500 ml") and saves them to the custom `quantity` attribute, so the frontend UI can display it neatly.

---

## 9. Error Handling
* **Backend Errors:** If SerpAPI fails or the key is wrong, the Python `try...except` block in `serp_service.py` catches it and returns an empty list `[]`. The route sees this empty list and returns a clean `{ "error": "No matching product found" }` with a 404 status. If no product query is passed, it safely returns 400.
* **Frontend Errors:** If `response.ok` is false (meaning the status code isn't 200), the frontend throws a Javascript error, catching the backend's message. The Catch block kicks in, hides the loading spinner, and displays the error message in red on the UI.

---

## 10. Deployment on Vercel (Serverless)
* Usually, a backend server constantly runs 24/7 waiting for requests.
* Vercel uses **Serverless Functions**. When you mapped `api/index.py` in `vercel.json`, you turned your Flask app into a lambda function.
* **How it works:** Vercel puts the backend to "sleep". When a user hits the endpoint, Vercel quickly spins up an instance just for that single request, runs the Python code, returns the JSON, and shuts down again. This is incredibly cost-effective.

---

## 11. Possible Interview Questions
1. How does your frontend communicate with the backend?
2. What is CORS and why did you need it in Flask?
3. If Google Shopping changes its title formats, how does it affect your app?
4. How did you optimize the SerpAPI rate limits?
5. Why did you choose `async/await` instead of `.then()` promises?
6. Tell me about Serverless architecture. What is a "cold start"?

---

## 12. Short Answers to Practice Speaking
* **Q: How does frontend talk to backend?**

# BudgetMart - Complete Interview Preparation Guide

This document is designed to help you confidently explain your BudgetMart project in a technical interview. It is structured to be beginner-friendly, easy to speak, and highly practical.

---

## 1. Complete Architecture and Flow

### Short Version (Elevator Pitch)
"BudgetMart is a full-stack web application. The frontend is built with HTML, CSS, and Vanilla JavaScript. When a user searches for a product, the frontend makes a REST API call to my Python Flask backend. The backend fetches real-time data using the Google Shopping API (via SerpAPI), cleans and normalizes the data, caches it to save API limits, and sends it back to the frontend, which dynamically renders the results on the screen."

### Detailed Version (Step-by-Step Flow)
1. **User input:** The user types a query like "Milk" into the frontend UI.
2. **Frontend:** JavaScript intercepts the search, shows a loading spinner, and sends an HTTP GET request using the `fetch` API.
3. **Backend:** The Flask server receives the request at the `/search` endpoint.
4. **Processing:** The backend checks the Cache. If it's a new query, it calls `serp_service.py` which reaches out to the external SerpAPI.
5. **External API:** SerpAPI scrapes Google Shopping and returns raw, messy data.
6. **Data Cleaning:** The backend uses Regular Expressions (Regex) to extract valid prices and quantities (like "500ml"), normalizing the data into a clean structure.
7. **Response:** Flask sends the clean data back as JSON.
8. **UI Update:** The frontend receives the JSON, loops through it, creating individual product cards on the screen dynamically.

---

## 2. Frontend Explanation

* **`index.html`:** The skeleton of the page. It contains the search bar, the sorting dropdown, and an empty container `div` where the generated results will go.
* **`style.css`:** The visual design. It handles the glassmorphism effects, responsive grid layouts so it looks good on mobile, and CSS animations so the product cards slide in smoothly.
* **`script.js`:** The brain of the frontend. It listens for the user clicking "Search", reads the input value, and makes the API call. 

**Core Concepts:**
* **Event Handling:** In JS, I use `addEventListener` to trigger functions when the user submits the form or changes the sort dropdown.
* **Fetch API & Async/Await:** Instead of reloading the whole page, I use `fetch()` to grab data behind the scenes. `async/await` forces the code to wait until the backend replies before trying to read the data, avoiding errors.
* **DOM Manipulation:** Once the JSON data arrives, JavaScript loops through it and creates new HTML elements (like `div` and `img`) entirely in memory, then attaches them to the DOM (the web page) all at once.

---

## 3. Backend Explanation (Flask)

**Structure:**
* **`index.py`:** The main entry point. It sets up the Flask application, enables CORS, connects the caching mechanism, and defines the API routes.
* **`services/serp_service.py`:** Contains the specific logic to talk to Google Shopping.
* **`services/normalizer.py`:** Contains helper logic to clean text strings and standardizes data.

**Routes:**
* **`/` (Home):** Returns a simple status check to confirm the backend is alive.
* **`/health`:** Used by deployment platforms to monitor if the server is healthy.
* **`/search`:** The core endpoint. It takes the user's query and returns the shopping data.

**Why Modular?**
Instead of putting all the code in `index.py` (which makes it massive and unreadable), I used a "Services" architecture. If I ever decide to switch from SerpAPI to a different API (like Amazon API), I only have to edit the `serp_service.py` file. The main server code doesn't break. This is called "Separation of Concerns."

---

## 4. `/search` Endpoint Deep Dive

Here is exactly what happens when the `/search` route is hit:
1. **Extraction:** It grabs the `product` and `location` parameters from the URL (e.g., `?product=bread`).
2. **Validation:** It checks if the search string is empty. If it is, it stops and sends back a `400 Bad Request` error.
3. **Caching:** It checks the Flask-Cache constraint. "Have I searched for 'bread' in the last 5 minutes?"
4. **API Call:** If not in cache, it triggers the function in `serp_service.py`.
5. **Response:** It takes the list of Python dictionaries returned by the service, converts them to JSON using `jsonify`, and sends a HTTP `200 OK` status back to the frontend.

---

## 5. SerpAPI Integration 

* **What it is:** Google Shopping doesn't have an official, free API. SerpAPI is a third-party service that legally scrapes Google search results and returns them in a structured JSON format.
* **How it works:** We make a request passing our `API_KEY`, the `query`, and the `location`. SerpAPI returns a massive JSON file.
* **Processing:** I slice only the top 12 results (so the app remains fast). I then selectively extract only the `source` (platform), `price`, and `thumbnail`, ignoring the useless data Google includes.

---

## 6. Caching (Flask-Caching)

* **Why it's needed:** SerpAPI limits the number of free searches I can do per month. Also, network calls are slow. 
* **How it works (Internals):** I use an in-memory cache (RAM). It works like a Dictionary (Key-Value store). 
  * *Key:* The exact URL used (e.g., `/search?product=Milk`).
  * *Value:* The final JSON response.
  * *Expiry (TTL):* Time To Live is set to 300 seconds (5 minutes).
* **Real-life example:** If 100 users search for "Amul Butter" at 10:00 AM, the first user's request takes 2 seconds to hit SerpAPI. The other 99 users get the result instantly (in milliseconds) from the server's cache, without using my API credits. At 10:05 AM, the cache deletes itself to fetch fresh prices next time.

---

## 7. Data Processing

* **Quantity Extraction using Regex:** Google Shopping titles are messy (e.g., "Amul Taaza Toned Milk - 500ml Pack of 2"). 
* Regex (Regular Expressions) is a tool to match text patterns. I wrote a Regex pattern to look for digits followed by "ml", "kg", "g", or "pieces". I wrote another to extract "Pack of X".
* **Why it's important:** Without this, the user has to read long messy titles. By extracting it, my frontend can display a clean, professional "Quantity badge" right next to the price.

---

## 8. Error Handling

* **Backend:** If the third-party SerpAPI goes down, my server could crash. I wrapped the API call in a `try...except` block, meaning if an error occurs, it safely catches it and returns an empty list, rather than crashing the whole site. I use proper status codes like `400` for bad input, and `404` for not found.
* **Frontend:** I wrap the `fetch` call in a `try...catch` block. If `response.ok` is false, it throws an error. The `catch` block catches this error, hides the rotating loading spinner, and makes a red error message box visible on the screen so the user knows what went wrong.

---

## 9. Deployment (Vercel Serverless)

Perfect! Now I have a complete understanding of the project. Let me create a comprehensive summary document. ## COMPREHENSIVE PROJECT ANALYSIS: BudgetMart --- ### **1. OVERALL PROJECT STRUCTURE** **Directory Layout:** ``` C:\BudgetMart/ ├── .claude/ # Claude Code settings │ └── settings.local.json ├── .venv/ # Python virtual environment ├── backend/ │ ├── .env # Backend environment variables │ ├── requirements.txt # Python dependencies │ ├── vercel.json # Vercel serverless configuration │ └── api/ │ ├── index.py # Main Flask application │ ├── requirements.txt # Duplicate dependencies for Vercel │ └── services/ │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── serp_service.py # Google Shopping API integration │ ├── normalizer.py # Data cleaning and normalization │ ├── bigbasket_service.py # Async BigBasket service (mock) │ ├── blinkit_service.py # Async Blinkit service (mock) │ └── instamart_service.py # Async Instamart service (mock) ├── frontend/ │ ├── index.html # Main HTML page │ ├── script.js # JavaScript frontend logic │ └── style.css # CSS styling ├── .env # Root environment variables (API key) ├── .git/ # Git repository ├── .gitignore ├── DEPLOYMENT.md # Vercel deployment guide ├── README.md # Empty directory (not used) ├── start_app.cmd # Windows batch script to start app ├── test_api.py # API testing script ├── interview_prep.txt # Interview preparation guide 1 ├── interviewprep2.txt # Interview preparation guide 2 └── tmp_test_*.py # Various temporary test files ``` --- ### **2. PROJECT PURPOSE & FEATURES** **Project Name:** BudgetMart **Description:** A real-time grocery price aggregator web application **Core Purpose:** BudgetMart is a full-stack web application that allows users to search for grocery products and instantly compare prices across multiple Indian e-commerce platforms (BigBasket, Blinkit, Swiggy Instamart) using real-time Google Shopping data. **Key Features:** 1. **Real-time Price Comparison** - Fetches live prices from Google Shopping via SerpAPI 2. **Multi-platform Support** - Aggregates data from multiple grocery delivery services 3. **Location-based Search** - Users can specify location (city, zip code) for relevant pricing 4. **Stock Availability Tracking** - Displays whether products are in stock or out of stock 5. **Quantity Extraction** - Automatically extracts product quantities (500ml, 1kg, etc.) from product titles using regex 6. **Smart Sorting** - Two sorting options: - Price: Low to High (with in-stock prioritization) - Availability First (in-stock items first) 7. **Best Price Highlighting** - Visual indicator for the cheapest in-stock item 8. **Product Images** - Displays product thumbnails from Google Shopping 9. **Caching** - 5-minute cache to optimize API usage and reduce latency 10. **Responsive Design** - Mobile-friendly interface with glassmorphism effects 11. **Error Handling** - Graceful error messages for failed searches or API issues --- ### **3. TECH STACK** **Frontend:** - **HTML5** - Structure and semantic markup - **CSS3** - Styling with glassmorphism, animations, responsive grid layouts - **Vanilla JavaScript** - DOM manipulation, event handling, async/await for API calls - **Font Awesome 6.4.0** - Icon library - **Google Fonts (Outfit)** - Typography **Backend:** - **Python 3.x** - Backend language - **Flask 3.0.2** - Lightweight web framework - **Flask-CORS 4.0.0** - Cross-Origin Resource Sharing for frontend-backend communication - **Flask-Caching 2.1.0** - In-memory caching system - **google-search-results (SerpApi Python client)** - Google Shopping API integration - **python-dotenv** - Environment variable management - **Gunicorn** - WSGI HTTP server - **Asyncio** - Asynchronous programming (for mock services) **Deployment:** - **Vercel** - Serverless function platform for both frontend and backend - **Vercel.json** - Configuration for serverless deployment **APIs & External Services:** - **SerpAPI** - Third-party Google Shopping scraper API (primary data source) - Configured for Indian market (gl=in, hl=en, location support) **Database:** - **No persistent database** - Data is fetched on-demand and cached in memory --- ### **4. BACKEND FILES - COMPLETE CONTENT** #### **File: C:\BudgetMart\backend\api\index.py** (Main Flask Application) ```python from flask import Flask, jsonify, request from flask_cors import CORS from flask_caching import Cache from api.services.serp_service import fetch_real_shopping_data app = Flask(__name__) CORS(app) # allows frontend to talk to backend # Cache configuration (5 minutes TTL for search results to avoid hitting SerpApi limits quickly) cache = Cache(app, config={'CACHE_TYPE': 'SimpleCache', 'CACHE_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT': 300}) @app.route("/") def home(): return jsonify({ "project": "BudgetMart", "status": "Backend Running Successfully 🚀 (SerpApi Enabled)" }) @app.route("/health") def health(): return jsonify({"status": "healthy"}), 200 # SEARCH API @app.route("/search") @cache.cached(query_string=True) def search(): query = request.args.get("product", "").strip() location = request.args.get("location", "India").strip() if not query: return jsonify({"error": "Please provide a search product"}), 400 print(f"Fetching real-time Google Shopping data for: {query} in {location}") # Execute the unified SerpApi fetch results = fetch_real_shopping_data(query, location) if not results: return jsonify({"error": "No matching product found or rate limit blocked"}), 404 return jsonify(results) if __name__ == "__main__": app.run(debug=True, port=5000) ``` **Purpose:** Entry point for the Flask application. Sets up: - CORS for cross-origin requests - In-memory SimpleCache with 300-second (5-minute) TTL - Three routes: home, health check, and search **Routes:** - `GET /` - Status check - `GET /health` - Health check for deployment monitoring - `GET /search?product=&location=` - Main search endpoint #### **File: C:\BudgetMart\backend\api\services\serp_service.py** (Google Shopping Integration) ```python from serpapi import GoogleSearch import asyncio import re import os from dotenv import load_dotenv load_dotenv() API_KEY = os.getenv("SERPAPI_KEY", "") def fetch_real_shopping_data(query: str, location: str = "India") -> list[dict]: """ Fetches real-time pricing and availability from Google Shopping using SerpApi. Supports a location string (e.g. "Mumbai", "110001", "India"). """ params = { "engine": "google_shopping", "q": query, "api_key": API_KEY, "gl": "in", "hl": "en", "location": location, } try: search = GoogleSearch(params) results = search.get_dict() shopping_results = results.get("shopping_results", []) parsed_data = [] for item in shopping_results[:12]: # Limit to top 12 results # Extract numbers from price if necessary, though extracted_price gives floats price = item.get("extracted_price") source = item.get("source", "Unknown Store") # Simple stock logic: if there's a price, it's generally in stock, unless explicitly stated otherwise # SerpApi doesn't always expose direct "OOS" unless in delivery text. We assume True. # Use direct merchant link if available, fallback to google shopping product link product_url = item.get("link") or item.get("product_link") or "#" # Extract quantity raw_title = item.get("title", query) found_qty = [] # Match packs/sets first pack_match = re.search(r'((?:pack|set)\s+of\s+\d+)', raw_title, re.IGNORECASE) if pack_match: found_qty.append(pack_match.group(1).title()) # Match standard units (weight/volume/pieces) weight_match = re.search(r'(\d+(?:\.\d+)?\s*(?:ml|l|litre|litres|g|kg|gm|piece|pieces|pc|pcs|unit|units))\b', raw_title, re.IGNORECASE) if weight_match: found_qty.append(weight_match.group(1).lower().strip()) quantity_str = " | ".join(found_qty) parsed_data.append({ "platform": source, "product_name": raw_title, "quantity": quantity_str, "price": price, "delivery": item.get("delivery", "Fees calculated at checkout"), "stock": True if price else False, "product_url": product_url, "image_url": item.get("thumbnail", "") }) return parsed_data except Exception as e: print(f"SerpApi Error: {e}") return [] ``` **Purpose:** Core service that fetches and normalizes real-time Google Shopping data **Key Logic:** - Calls SerpAPI with Google Shopping engine - Extracts top 12 results - Parses each item to extract: - Price (float) - Platform/Source name - Product title - Delivery information - Product URL and thumbnail image - Uses regex to extract quantity information from product titles - Returns cleaned, normalized data structure **Regex Patterns Used:** 1. Pack matching: `r'((?:pack|set)\s+of\s+\d+)'` - finds "pack of X" or "set of X" 2. Weight/Volume matching: `r'(\d+(?:\.\d+)?\s*(?:ml|l|litre|litres|g|kg|gm|piece|pieces|pc|pcs|unit|units))\b'` - finds quantities with units #### **File: C:\BudgetMart\backend\api\services\normalizer.py** (Data Normalization) ```python import re def normalize_product_name(query: str) -> str: """ Normalizes a product name by lowercasing, stripping extra whitespace, and removing common filler words or standardizing units. """ query = query.lower().strip() # Replace common variations query = query.replace("1/2 litre", "500ml") query = query.replace("half litre", "500ml") query = query.replace("1 kg", "1000g") # Remove extra spaces query = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', query) return query def generate_mock_data(query: str, platform_name: str, base_price: float, stock_chance: float = 0.9) -> dict: """ Generates realistic mock data for the given platform. """ import random # Simulate an Out of Stock scenario is_in_stock = random.random() < stock_chance # Introduce small price variations between platforms (+/- 10%) price_variation = base_price * random.uniform(-0.1, 0.1) final_price = round(base_price + price_variation) return { "platform": platform_name, "product_name": query.title(), "price": final_price if is_in_stock else None, "stock": is_in_stock, "product_url": f"https://www.{platform_name.lower()}.com/search?q={query.replace(' ', '+')}" } def get_base_pricing(normalized_query: str) -> float: """ Determine a basic authentic fake base price based on item keywords. """ db = { "milk": 30, "amul milk": 33, "bread": 40, "britannia bread": 45, "butter": 55, "amul butter": 60, "eggs": 70, "coffee": 150, "nescafe": 200, "maggi": 14, "yippee": 12, "rice": 60, "basmati rice": 120, "flour": 40, "sugar": 45, "salt": 25, "tata salt": 28, "chips": 20, "lays": 20, "kurkure": 20, "coke": 40, "pepsi": 38 } # Try exact match if normalized_query in db: return db[normalized_query] # Try partial match for key, price in db.items(): if key in normalized_query: return price # Default base price for an unknown item import random return random.randint(30, 250) ``` **Purpose:** Helper functions for data normalization (though these are currently used by mock services, not the main SerpAPI service) **Functions:** 1. `normalize_product_name()` - Normalizes queries by standardizing units and whitespace 2. `generate_mock_data()` - Generates realistic mock product data 3. `get_base_pricing()` - Returns estimated base prices based on product keywords #### **File: C:\BudgetMart\backend\api\services\bigbasket_service.py** (Mock BigBasket Service) ```python import asyncio import random from .normalizer import get_base_pricing, generate_mock_data async def fetch_bigbasket_data(query: str, normalized_query: str) -> dict: """ Simulates async scraping from BigBasket. """ # Simulate network/scraping delay (0.8s to 2.2s) await asyncio.sleep(random.uniform(0.8, 2.2)) base_price = get_base_pricing(normalized_query) # BigBasket might have a slightly different stock chance data = generate_mock_data(query, "BigBasket", base_price, stock_chance=0.95) return data ``` **Purpose:** Mock asynchronous service for BigBasket (currently unused - not integrated into main search) **Note:** This is a placeholder for future integration with BigBasket scraping. SerpAPI (Google Shopping)** - **Base URL:** `https://api.serpapi.com/search` - **Authentication:** API Key in headers/params - **Purpose:** Scrape Google Shopping results for price comparisons - **Key Parameters:** - `engine`: "google_shopping" - `q`: Product query - `gl`: "in" (India region) - `hl`: "en" (English language) - `location`: Optional location for localized results - **Response:** JSON with shopping results, prices, images, delivery info - **Rate Limits:** Limited by plan (free tier has monthly limits) - **Data Points Extracted:** - `extracted_price` - Float price - `source` - Platform name - `title` - Product name - `link` - Product URL - `thumbnail` - Product image - `delivery` - Delivery info --- ### **10. KEY FEATURES BREAKDOWN** **Real-time Data Fetching:** - Uses SerpAPI to fetch live Google Shopping results - No stale data - every search hits live API (unless cached) - Location-aware pricing for India **Caching Strategy:** - 5-minute TTL (SimpleCache in RAM) - Reduces API costs significantly - Improves user experience (cached results load instantly) - Cache key: URL query string **Quantity Extraction:** - Two-phase regex matching: 1. Pack/Set detection: `r'((?:pack|set)\s+of\s+\d+)'` 2. Weight/Volume detection: `r'(\d+(?:\.\d+)?\s*(?:ml|l|litre|litres|g|kg|gm|piece|pieces|pc|pcs|unit|units))\b'` - Combines multiple matches (e.g., "Pack Of 2 | 500 ml") **Sorting Options:** 1. **Price Ascending** - Lowest to highest, but in-stock prioritized 2. **Availability First** - In-stock items first, then others **Best Price Highlighting:** - Identifies cheapest in-stock item - Visual indicator: green border + "BEST PRICE" badge - Text color changes to green **Stock Status Logic:** - If SerpAPI returns a price → In Stock (True) - If no price → Out of Stock (False) - Grayscale + reduced opacity for OOS items **Product Cards Display:** - Product image (if available) - Platform name (with hash-based color) - Product title - Stock status badge - Quantity badge (if extracted) - Price in Indian Rupees (₹) - Delivery information - Direct link to store (target="_blank") --- ### **SUMMARY** **BudgetMart** is a full-stack, real-time grocery price comparison application built with: - **Frontend:** HTML5, CSS3 (glassmorphism + animations), Vanilla JavaScript (async/await, DOM manipulation) - **Backend:** Python Flask with modular services, CORS enabled, caching via Flask-Caching - **External API:** SerpAPI for Google Shopping data - **Deployment:** Vercel serverless functions (both frontend and backend) - **Data Processing:** Regex for quantity extraction, JSON normalization - **UX Features:** Responsive design, dark theme, price highlighting, stock filtering, location search The application follows a clean architecture with separation of concerns (services pattern) and is optimized for cost-efficiency through intelligent caching while providing real-time price comparisons across Indian grocery delivery platforms.
